content.text: Images are the most bandwidth-intensive resource on the web, and media elements — video and audio — are among the most complex. Getting them right from the start means understanding not just the HTML attributes but also the responsive image model, format selection, lazy loading, and how Angular's NgOptimizedImage directive automates the best practices.

attrs.alt: HTML images and media elements — img anatomy, srcset/sizes, picture art direction, and Angular NgOptimizedImage
attrs.title: HTML Images & Media Elements

content.text: HTML provides dedicated elements for embedding images, video, and audio:
for audio. Grouping an image with a caption uses
. None of these elements require JavaScript — the browser handles loading, decoding, and playback natively.
. The remaining attributes control layout stability, loading behaviour, and decoding.
Image source. Required. Use WebP or AVIF for best compression.
Screen-reader text. Empty string for decorative images. Never omit the attribute.
Reserve layout space. Prevents Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
"lazy" defers off-screen images until near the viewport. Use "eager" (or omit) for above-the-fold images.
"async" lets the browser decode off the main thread — smoother rendering.
"high" on the LCP image tells the browser to load it first.
should always be the intrinsic dimensions of the image in pixels (no CSS units). The browser uses them to calculate the aspect ratio before the image loads, which eliminates layout shift.
attribute must be present on every
— but its value depends on whether the image conveys information.

Rule of thumb: if you removed the image and replaced it with the alt text, would the page still convey the same information? If yes, the alt text is good. If the image is purely visual decoration, alt="" is correct — do not write alt="decorative" or alt="image".
groups an image (or code block, diagram, or table) with its
. The caption is visible text and is not a replacement for

Screen readers announce the caption text in addition to the alt text, so keep them complementary rather than identical. The alt describes the image content; the caption provides context (figure number, source, interpretation).
Responsive images with srcset and sizes
A single image served at a fixed pixel width wastes bandwidth on small screens and looks blurry on high-DPR displays. The
attribute lists candidate images with their intrinsic widths, and
tells the browser how wide the image will be rendered at each breakpoint. The browser then picks the smallest image that fully covers the rendered size times the device pixel ratio.
is evaluated left-to-right; the first matching media condition wins. The last value (
above) is the default — no media condition needed. Always keep
as a valid fallback for browsers that do not support
The picture element and art direction
goes beyond srcset by letting you serve completely different images — not just different sizes — per breakpoint or per supported format. It contains one or more
elements followed by a required fallback
element. The browser uses the first
lets the browser skip formats it cannot decode without making a network request.
embeds a player with playback controls. Specify multiple
children in format preference order — the browser plays the first one it supports.

<video
  width="854"
  height="480"
  controls
  preload="metadata"
  poster="thumbnail.webp"
>
  <source src="lesson.webm" type="video/webm" />
  <source src="lesson.mp4"  type="video/mp4" />
  <!-- Captions for accessibility -->
  <track
    kind="captions"
    src="lesson-en.vtt"
    srclang="en"
    label="English"
    default
  />
  Your browser does not support the video element.
</video>
renders the browser's native playback UI.
fetches only the duration and first frame — use
if the video is below the fold, or
if it is the main page content.
is required for WCAG 1.2.2 compliance (captions for pre-recorded audio content).

<!-- Audio player -->
<audio controls preload="metadata">
  <source src="lesson-audio.ogg" type="audio/ogg" />
  <source src="lesson-audio.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" />
  Your browser does not support audio.
</audio>

and automatically applies image best practices. It generates a

mode the parent element must have
in fill mode — NgOptimizedImage manages sizing via CSS. Use fill for hero sections, card thumbnails, and any image that should scale to its container.

Images are the primary driver of two Core Web Vitals: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP — load speed) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS — visual stability). The table below maps each attribute to its performance impact.
Browser reserves exact space before image loads, eliminating reflow.
Defers network requests until the image is near the viewport.
Moves the LCP image to the top of the network queue.
Smaller images on small screens reduce bytes transferred.
25-50% smaller than JPEG/PNG at equivalent quality.
Decoding off the main thread reduces jank on image-heavy pages.
Combines fetchpriority=high, loading=eager, and preconnect in one attribute.
) for purely decorative images so screen readers skip them.
Write alt text that conveys the information or function of the image — not the file name or literal description of pixels. "Graph showing a 40% drop in page load time" is better than "graph.png".
for audio descriptions of visual content (WCAG 1.2.5).
Avoid autoplay with sound. If autoplay is needed for design reasons, set the muted attribute — unmuted autoplay is blocked by browsers by default and is disorienting for screen reader users.
Color contrast matters for image-based text. Do not embed the only version of important text inside an image — it cannot be resized, translated, or selected by users with low vision.
Building an Angular image gallery component
A gallery component is a common pattern: a list of thumbnails that renders efficiently with lazy loading and clean alt text derived from data.
on all gallery thumbnails because none of them carry the
Test that images bind the correct src, alt, and dimensions. With NgOptimizedImage, use
to query the directive's input in the DOM.
Image formats — choosing the right one
The image format you choose determines file size, quality, and browser support. The modern recommendation is to serve WebP or AVIF wrapped in a picture element with JPEG or PNG as fallback.
Lossy compression. Smallest for photographic content at equivalent quality.
Lossless. Larger than JPEG for photos; great for flat-color images.
25-35% smaller than JPEG/PNG. Supported by all modern browsers.
Largest quality gains (50% smaller than JPEG). Excellent browser support as of 2024.
Vector format — resolution independent. Inline-able in HTML. Not for photos.
Replaced by video/webm or CSS animation for most use cases.
. Image CDNs (Cloudinary, Imgix, Cloudflare Images) handle format conversion automatically based on the Accept header.
Skeleton loading and blur-up placeholder patterns

When images load lazily there is a visual gap between the placeholder box and the final image appearing. Two common patterns bridge this gap: a CSS skeleton animation on the container, or a low-quality image placeholder (LQIP) that cross-fades to the full image. Both use the Host element and Angular's built-in event bindings on the img element.
event fires when the browser finishes decoding the image. Setting
triggers Angular change detection, which adds the
class and starts the CSS opacity transition. This avoids a dependency on JavaScript image loading libraries for a simple fade-in.
attribute. Browsers show a broken-image icon; screen readers announce the file name. Every
, even if the value is empty.
. The browser cannot reserve space before the image loads, causing layout shift as images pop in.
on the LCP image. This delays the most important paint. Only lazy-load images that are below the fold.
Serving oversized images. A 4000px image scaled to 400px in CSS still downloads 4000px worth of data. Use srcset or an image CDN to serve appropriately sized files.
. Pre-recorded audio content must have captions to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA.

lets the browser pick the right file size;
enables art direction and format negotiation.
(ngSrc directive) automates srcset generation, fetchpriority, lazy loading, and dev-time dimension warnings.
Serve WebP or AVIF for the best compression; provide JPEG/PNG fallbacks inside picture source elements for older browsers.
